headline: Welcome to Week 1: Where Dominant Pitching Gets Humiliated and Zach Neto Carries the Whole Building on His Back
subheadline: The Buttville Skidmarks posted a 0.17 WHIP and still lost, the General Hospital–Wonderdogs rivalry is already must-watch television, and the Sugar Land Skeeters are the class of the league before March is even over.
body:
## Thriller of the Week

### General Hospital vs. Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs — 4-4 Tie

Let me set the scene. **General Hospital** rolls into Week 1 with what might be the most complete roster in the league — a .414 OBP (best in the league, and we'll get to that), 6 HR, 23 RBI, and three quality starts. This is the fantasy equivalent of the 2004 Red Sox showing up to spring training and announcing they are built different. And they *tie*. They tie a team named after a Bob's Burgers location. (I mean that as a compliment to the **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs**, who are absolutely earning the right to be taken seriously.)

The Wonderdogs, for their part, countered with 7 HR, a 2.98 ERA, three quality starts of their own, and Donovan going multi-HR in a week where they were supposedly the underdogs. This is the kind of matchup where both managers are staring at their phones at 11:59 PM Sunday night frantically refreshing the scoring page and both end up feeling like they lost. Nobody won. Nobody lost. Both teams are probably furious. That's great television.

The stakes going forward are real. **General Hospital** is a legitimate title contender and dropping a half-point on what should have been a winnable matchup against a team ranked seven spots below them in these power rankings is going to matter in September. Meanwhile, the **Wonderdogs** just proved they can hang with the best team in the league. Mark this one on your calendar — this is shaping up as the league's defining rivalry, and we're one week in.

---

## The Week's Defining Moment

Category fantasy baseball has a villain, and its name is "uneven production distribution." The **Buttville Skidmarks** just became its most recent victim, and the crime scene is genuinely beautiful in the way only a perfect fantasy tragedy can be.

Here's what happened: Kevin Gausman — *Kevin Gausman* — posted a 0.17 WHIP over 6.0 innings. Let me repeat that. Zero point one seven. That is not a typo. For context, that's the kind of number you'd expect from a video game set to "Little League" difficulty. It is the most dominant single-pitcher performance of the young season in this league. Chase DeLauter hit 4 HR and drove in 5 runs. The **Skidmarks** put up 47 strikeouts on the week — the most in the entire league — and logged 40.1 innings pitched. They had 7 HR, a legitimate power display. And they lost 7-3 to the **Benchwarmers**. 

The **Benchwarmers** posted zero quality starts. *Zero.* Their ERA was 4.50. Their OBP was .314. Their entire power production for the week was 2 HR. If you showed me these two stat lines blind and asked me which team won, I would bet my entire record collection on the Skidmarks every single time, and I would be wrong. What happened is the most pure distillation of roto-style category baseball's cruelest feature: it doesn't matter HOW dominant your production is, it matters HOW MANY categories that production covers. Gausman's 0.17 WHIP won them the WHIP category. That's one category. DeLauter's 4 HR helped win the HR category. That's two. But the **Benchwarmers** were quietly competent across enough other categories — runs, hits, OBP, saves — that they walked out with a 7-3 record on the matchup like they belonged there.

Zach Neto was the engine. A 6-run, 2-HR explosion from one player on a team this thin is the difference between losing 7-3 and losing 9-1. Neto is a genuine star, full stop. But the **Skidmarks** should be having a serious organizational meeting right now, because if Gausman pitching a near-perfect game can't get you a win against the league's 14th-ranked team, you have a structural problem that individual brilliance cannot solve.

---

## Matchup Deep-Dives

### Sugar Land Skeeters def. Riverside Roughnecks, 9-1

This wasn't a matchup, it was a nature documentary. The **Sugar Land Skeeters** won nine categories and posted 8 HR (tied for the league lead), 21 RBI, and a .366 OBP while the **Roughnecks** rotation imploded to a 7.91 ERA. Schanuel, Abreu, Happ, and Baldwin all going multi-HR in the same week is the fantasy equivalent of four wide receivers each going for 100 yards — you don't see it often, and when you do, the opposing manager is just trying to survive. The only silver lining for **Riverside** is that Langeliers showed real power and Royce Lewis gave them something to build around offensively. But a 7.91 ERA from a rotation in Week 1 is not a small sample size anomaly — it's a five-alarm fire that needs to be addressed immediately or this team will be fighting for lottery position before May.

### BaseballPro77 def. Canberra Wombats, 8-2

**BaseballPro77** came out swinging with 8 HR (tied for the league lead), 20 runs scored, and Murakami going three-deep while Trout added two more. When your lineup has that kind of ceiling — and Stanton chipping in on top of it — you can survive a 4.25 ERA from the rotation because your offense is essentially a high-powered offense that also happens to play baseball. The **Canberra Wombats**, meanwhile, posted zero home runs in a week where the league average was approximately "a lot," and Dylan Cease's genuinely solid 0.94 WHIP was completely wasted because Logan Webb decided to have an absolute nightmare outing. Zero HR is not sustainable in this field. Zero HR is a philosophical problem, not a roster management problem. You have to go get a power bat, full stop.

### North Correa def. Wisconsin Rapids Reapers, 7-3

**North Correa** looks like a legitimate contender, and this matchup told you exactly why: 23 runs scored (league high), a 2.73 ERA from the rotation, two quality starts, and four stolen bases from a team that has speed weapons that other rosters simply don't. Arozarena and Hoerner in the same lineup is the kind of speed tandem that will quietly drain categories from opponents all season. The **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers**, on the other hand, have a Matthew Boyd situation that needs to be resolved immediately — a 14.73 ERA from your rotation's back-end arm is the kind of stat line that makes opponents feel like they're cheating. Dropping McGreevy without adding meaningful pitching help is a move that raises real questions about the front office's offseason plan.

### Albuquerque Bombers def. Abbotsford-Colby Twins, 6-4

This was the matchup where **Albuquerque** proved you can win a category matchup without hitting a single home run — a feat that sounds impossible and shouldn't be attempted at home. Alcantara and Rogers both posted quality starts with WHIPs under 1.00, and Yelich's .600 OBP is a number I had to look at twice before believing it. You don't need homers when your pitching staff is just systematically suffocating opposing offenses. The **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** had real contributions — 6 HR, 5 SB, and 43 strikeouts from the staff — but Detmers and Peralta both getting roughed up for a combined 6.00 ERA turned a winnable matchup into a loss. This roster has a high ceiling and a genuinely low floor when the pitching staff decides not to show up, which is the exact thing that gets managers eliminated in October.

### Athletic Park Dragons def. Hiroshima Radiation, 5-4

This is the matchup that's going to haunt **Hiroshima Radiation** for weeks. They posted a 2.35 ERA — the *best in the league this week* — and 44 strikeouts, and they still lost. Their offense generated zero stolen bases and a .304 OBP against a **Dragons** team whose own offense was posting a .301 OBP and relying almost entirely on Sánchez's 0.50 WHIP and Joe Ryan's 0.56 WHIP to stay competitive. When both teams are pitching brilliantly and neither offense is doing much, it comes down to the margins — and **Athletic Park** won those margins 5-4. **Hiroshima** is a team with a real identity and real pitching, but zero stolen bases is going to cost them categories all season long unless they find a speed weapon somewhere.

### Benchwarmers def. Buttville Skidmarks, 7-3

*(See above. We gave this one its own section. It deserved it.)*

### General Hospital 4 — Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs 4 (TIE)

*(See Thriller of the Week. We're not going to make you read it twice.)*

---

## Lucky/Unlucky Team of the Week

**Lucky Team: Benchwarmers.** Zero quality starts. A 4.50 ERA. A .314 OBP. Two home runs from the entire roster for the entire week. By any objective measure, the **Benchwarmers** are the exact kind of team that should be sending their opponent a polite concession email by Thursday. Instead, Zach Neto went nuclear and carried this team to a 7-3 win that should have been a comfortable loss. The fantasy gods were smiling on them this week. Whether those gods will still be paying attention in Week 8 is a different question.

**Unlucky Team: Buttville Skidmarks.** A 0.17 WHIP from Gausman. Seven home runs. Forty-seven strikeouts. Five runs from DeLauter alone. There are teams in this league who would post those numbers and throw a parade. The **Skidmarks** posted those numbers and got a 7-3 loss and a spot near the bottom of the power rankings. This is not a talent problem — the talent is clearly there. This is a category coverage problem, and it's the kind of problem that requires roster surgery, not just roster maintenance.

---

## League Record Watch

**General Hospital** tied their own league record with a .414 OBP this week — a mark that, to absolutely nobody's surprise, was previously set by **General Hospital** in Week 1. (This is the fantasy equivalent of a team breaking their own record in the exact same game they set it. Somehow on brand.) No new records were broken this week, but with **BaseballPro77** posting 8 HR and **North Correa** scoring 23 runs, the record book is going to get an aggressive workout over the next few months.

---

## Manager Spotlight: Sugar Land Skeeters

The **Sugar Land Skeeters** are 1-0 and sitting at the top of these power rankings, and based on what we saw in Week 1, it's not close. A 9-1 demolition of a team that was supposed to be competitive, with nine category wins, is the kind of performance that sends a message to the entire league: *figure out a plan, because we're ready right now.*

What makes this team genuinely scary isn't any single player — it's the depth. Schanuel, Abreu, Happ, and Baldwin all going multi-HR in the same week doesn't happen by accident. That's roster construction. That's a manager who built a lineup with power at every turn of the order so that when the offense gets hot, it gets *historically* hot. Tied for the league lead in HR with 8 this week, second in RBI with 21, and a .366 OBP from a lineup that clearly doesn't need to scratch and claw for runs — this team wins with volume.

The rotation isn't elite — a 4.13 ERA is fine but not dominant — but in a league where offensive firepower often determines matchups, this team may not need an elite rotation. They need to win categories, and they are winning categories. The playoff outlook from this vantage point is as good as anyone's in the league. If they can tighten up the pitching or add a genuine ace, this team becomes a genuine championship-level threat. Right now, they're the team everyone else is building toward.

---

## Trade Value Watch

**Zach Neto, Benchwarmers** — Neto delivered a 6-run, 2-HR explosion for a team that desperately needed every one of those contributions this week. **BUY.** He's clearly the best player on his roster and he's producing at a level that should make other managers nervous. The problem is his team might not be competitive enough to keep him, which means he could be on the trade block before the deadline. Get ahead of it.

**Kevin Gausman, Buttville Skidmarks** — A 0.17 WHIP over 6 innings is the kind of performance that makes every manager in the league immediately open the trade chat. **HOLD/BUY.** If the Skidmarks are going to struggle to convert elite pitching into wins, their manager might be willing to sell low on Gausman out of frustration. That would be a mistake on their part and an opportunity for everyone else.

**Chase DeLauter, Buttville Skidmarks** — Four home runs and five RBI in a losing effort is genuinely impressive and genuinely brutal at the same time. **HOLD.** There's real power here, but if the **Skidmarks** are going to be sellers as they figure out their category balance issues, DeLauter becomes an interesting acquisition target for a team that needs pop in the middle of their lineup.

---

## Power Rankings

1. **Sugar Land Skeeters** | Nine category wins and a 9-1 demolition of the Roughnecks — Schanuel, Abreu, Happ, and Baldwin all went multi-HR in the same week, making this the deepest and most balanced offensive roster in the league [SAME]
2. **BaseballPro77** | An 8-2 blowout win powered by Murakami's 3 HR, Trout's 2 HR, and Stanton contributing — the rotation remains inconsistent but the sheer offensive ceiling here is unmatched when everyone produces together [UP]
3. **General Hospital** | A 4-4 tie against a Wonderdogs team they should have beaten stings, but a .414 OBP, 6 HR, 23 RBI, and 3 QS all in one week confirms this is a complete roster firing on nearly every cylinder [SAME]
4. **Albuquerque Bombers** | A 6-4 win built on pitching efficiency — Alcantara and Rogers each delivered quality starts with WHIPs under 1.00, and Yelich's .600 OBP anchored a lineup that won without hitting a single home run [UP]
5. **North Correa** | A convincing 7-3 win with 23 runs scored and four stolen bases; Arozarena and Hoerner are difference-makers in the speed categories and the rotation posted a 2.73 ERA with two quality starts [SAME]
6. **Athletic Park Dragons** | A 5-4 win that required every ounce of their pitching staff's performance — Sánchez's 0.50 WHIP and Joe Ryan's 0.56 WHIP are elite, but the offense's .301 OBP is dangerously thin for sustained success [SAME]
7. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | A 4-4 tie against General Hospital is genuinely impressive given the firepower they faced, and Donovan's 2 HR plus three quality starts show real roster depth, but ties don't move standings [UP]
8. **Hiroshima Radiation** | Lost 5-4 in a tight battle and still posted a 2.35 ERA and 44 strikeouts as a staff — this team keeps pitching well enough to win and keeps finding ways not to; the offense's 0 SB and .304 OBP are the culprits [DOWN]
9. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | Lost 6-4 despite 6 HR and 5 SB — the pitching staff imploded with a 6.00 ERA and zero quality starts, and Detmers and Peralta both got roughed up; this roster's floor is worryingly low when the arms fail [DOWN]
10. **Riverside Roughnecks** | Got obliterated 9-1 by the Skeeters and the pitching staff's 7.91 ERA tells the whole story; Langeliers' 3 HR and Royce Lewis's 2 HR are encouraging offensive signs but the rotation is in crisis [DOWN]
11. **Canberra Wombats** | Lost 8-2 and posted zero HR in a power-heavy league — Dylan Cease's 0.94 WHIP was wasted, Logan Webb's 10.80 ERA was disqualifying, and a roster without home run hitters cannot compete in this field [SAME]
12. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | Lost 7-3 and the rotation posted a 5.79 ERA with Matthew Boyd's 14.73 ERA being genuinely alarming; dropping McGreevy without a meaningful replacement shows a front office still searching for answers [SAME]
13. **Buttville Skidmarks** | Gausman's 0.17 WHIP and DeLauter's 4 HR were spectacular individual performances that still produced a 7-3 loss — category imbalance is this team's defining flaw and it cost them a winnable matchup [DOWN]
14. **Benchwarmers** | Won 7-3 on fumes — zero quality starts, a 4.50 ERA, and a 23-hit week is not a formula for sustained success; Zach Neto is a genuine star but one player cannot carry this roster into the playoffs [SAME]

---

## Waiver Wire

**[B] Albuquerque Bombers** — Added Keaton Winn, Noelvi Marte, Braxton Garrett, Josh Jung; dropped Sam Antonacci, Jonathan Loáisiga — A four-player haul shows aggressive roster construction from a manager who isn't content to just win in Week 1 and coast. Jung and Marte add infield flexibility and real upside, while Winn and Garrett give rotation depth behind what is already a pretty good one-two punch. Dropping Loáisiga is the one move that raises an eyebrow given how thin bullpen depth can get in a long season.

**[B+] Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** — Added Chase Dollander, Justin Bruihl, Erik Miller, Ildemaro Vargas; dropped Jack Flaherty, Justin Sterner, Taylor Clarke, Matt Strahm — A complete bullpen and depth overhaul from a team that just went 4-4 against the best roster in the league, which tells me the manager saw something in that matchup and decided to fix it immediately. Dollander is the kind of high-upside arm that rewards patience. The only thing that stings here is dropping Strahm the same week **BaseballPro77** picked him up. That's the kind of missed read that gets you in Week 12.

**[A-] General Hospital** — Added Kumar Rocker; dropped Jack Leiter, Janson Junk — If Rocker is healthy and getting stretched out as a starter, this is one of the best moves of the week in any category. You're swapping two marginal arms for a legitimate upside play on a rotation that is already posting a .95 WHIP as a staff. The ceiling here becomes genuinely scary. The risk is real — injury history is not nothing — but the reward absolutely justifies it.

**[D] Benchwarmers** — Added Casey Schmitt; dropped Josh Naylor, Max Scherzer, Javier Assad — I'm going to need the **Benchwarmers** manager to explain this one slowly and with visual aids. You just won 7-3 on the back of a power shortage and zero quality starts, and your response is to drop Naylor (one of the better power bats available), Scherzer, and Assad while adding Casey Schmitt? This isn't roster management, this is an act of self-sabotage. Every problem this team has just got measurably worse.

**[C-] Canberra Wombats** — Added JR Ritchie; dropped Roki Sasaki — With all due respect to JR Ritchie as a human being and aspiring baseball player, he is not Roki Sasaki. Sasaki is a legitimate ace-ceiling arm in a league where good pitching is currency. Ritchie is a lottery ticket you find under a seat cushion. This decision compounds an already shaky rotation situation for a team that posted zero HR this week and desperately needs things to go right everywhere else.

**[B] North Correa** — Added Austin Wells; dropped J.T. Realmuto — This is just smart, forward-looking roster management. Realmuto is a legendary name but the ascending Wells has more multi-category upside at a position that doesn't usually give you much. For a team already in the playoff conversation, this is exactly the kind of incremental upgrade that wins championships in October.

**[C] Athletic Park Dragons** — Added Dalton Rushing; dropped Colton Cowser — Cowser was useful. Rushing is unproven. Dropping a known contributor for a speculative gamble on a player who hasn't established himself at the MLB level feels like a move that weakens the outfield right now for uncertain gains down the road. Maybe it works out. But maybe is doing a lot of heavy lifting in that sentence.
